IRIAM Streaming Notes: Streamed for 1 hour 17 minutes, starting at 1 pm EST, doing an incentive for points for an event. (Doing art for points for the card event) Not very many people joined, but I welcomed everyone in regardless and talked to them. I did notice I was too focused on setting up my art and not engaging with viewers, something I want to improve on. Got a gift from one viewer who hit a point goal and will work on art for them.
X (Twitter) Posting Notes: I do my best to schedule posts ahead of time, and spend a good few minutes throughout the day interacting with posts, such as giving likes, retweeting, and replying. If I don't have something dedicated to post, I'll just do a text post about my day, something I find interesting, or a random photo of something I want to share.
X (Twitter) Interaction Notes: I typically stick to liking and retweeting posts, and usually only really reply to posts of people I know. I'm trying to branch out and reply to more people, and trying to make connections.
Reflection / Comments (Optional):
Having a shared interest can help out with creating conversations, such as talking about a musician that we like. I think I should try bringing up things I know and like, and not be afraid to talk at length about them. I haven't talked about badges yet, as no one has asked about them or achieved any yet, and I'm not yet sure how to approach the topic without it sounding like I'm pressuring anyone. I also notice that the time slot I choose might not be the best, at least for some days. I'm thinking about potentially changing and trying out some times around the afternoon. 
I also think I should try making posts that encourage engagement and interactions. Also would like to try and discover more ways to grow my audience on social media platforms. I feel I have a basic grasp on it, but could benefit from learning more.
Which Viewer Support Practices did you a: I shared my current goal with viewers during my stream, I ran a mini-event stream involving gifting or support
Notes on Viewer Support Practices: I notice every viewer that comes in, and thank them for their support. I've even been able to notice those who have appeared for more than one stream. I find that we have a pretty good rapport, but I feel there are times where I'm not interacting with them as much as I should. That's something I would like to improve on.
Current Viewer Support Focus Goal: I want to improve taking incentive in interacting with viewers. I've noticed when I'm in a conversation setting, I usually only speak when I've been spoken to or after someone has shared something and I feel I can contribute to it. I want to be able to start conversations without needing to be prompted, and be comfortable with bringing up topics even if they feel to "weird" (like talking about niche interests)

IRIAM Streaming Notes: I streamed twice today. Once at 11:30 am and Another at around 8pm Cst. Both times I chatted with the few viewers that popped in, while also writing and talking about various topics from New movies coming out to the weather that's been hitting the US.

Mostly I talked about me being silly and enjoying my time on IRIAM, and inviting others to join my streams.  I also followed a few other people who were stating they were debuting soon on IRIAM, liked and reposted their Early Access posts.  

Replies and interactions mostly was of a few people commenting on my posts with good vibes, and stating they believe in me. (Which was nice!)

Reflection / Comments (Optional):
Today was a bit rough (not only cause my internet went out, yay tech issues.) But also compared to the other streams I've done it wasn't as much interaction as I usually get, but I also know there are days that are gonna be like that. Today even though I did stream for a few hours, very few people came in, talked, or interacted other than a couple people (my partner and a couple friends.)  who I knew before joining IRIAM. 

 I'm also the kind of person who wants to show support to others, but also not stretch myself thin (which I'm bad about), so mostly right now it's finding that right balance of support without pushing myself too hard. Cause the last thing I want or need is burnout. 

Past all that though I've loved who all I've interacted with and met so far on IRIAM, and I hope to grow more not only to improve what I do, but also to eventually help others more from what I learn from it.

IRIAM Streaming Notes:
Today I streamed for approximately 4 hours total.

I hosted a 2-hour outdoor walking stream where I interacted with viewers while exploring my neighborhood, visiting local stores, getting boba, and grabbing Chipotle. It felt very natural and conversational, and I focused on maintaining engagement through casual storytelling and check-ins.

Later in the evening, I ran another 2-hour segment of “Lumo’s Starnight Radio,” which centered around relaxed conversation and one-on-one engagement. The format allowed for deeper discussion and strong viewer connection.

Overall, today focused heavily on consistent interaction, conversational pacing, and building comfort in longer stream blocks.
X (Twitter) Posting Notes: Posted updates about today’s outdoor stream and evening Starnight Radio session. Continued engaging with community members and maintaining active presence through replies and interactions.
X (Twitter) Interaction Notes: Engaged with posts from fellow streamers, liked and replied to 2 of Lock Heavenly's Posts, and tens of over Vtuber's and artists, and maintained visibility within the IRIAM and Twitter community space.
Reflection / Comments (Optional):
What went well today was my ability to stay present and committed during longer stream blocks. I experimented with a more relaxed, connection-focused format and it felt sustainable.

I’d like to continue improving my consistency across multiple days per week while balancing energy and outside responsibilities. Moving forward, I want to focus on maintaining both streaming frequency and quality interaction.
Which Viewer Support Practices did you a: I shared my current goal with viewers during my stream, I consciously focused on viewer interaction during my stream
Notes on Viewer Support Practices:
Today I focused heavily on natural interaction and maintaining strong conversational flow, especially during my outdoor stream and Starnight Radio segment. Even with a smaller live audience, I prioritized making the stream feel personal and welcoming.

I consciously slowed down and allowed space for deeper discussion instead of trying to overfill silence. This helped the stream feel more intentional and comfortable.

I also made sure to acknowledge and appreciate engagement consistently, reinforcing that every viewer interaction matters.
Current Viewer Support Focus Goal: Celebrate support moments more consistently

IRIAM Streaming Notes:
Stream Time: 1:35:38

This is the report for Saturday, February 11th. I thought I had submitted this report already last night, but it doesn't seem to have gone through, so I'm submitting it again. Anyway, on Saturday I made idle conversation with chat. I asked about their favorite video games and tried to find a video game we could all play together. A couple of people said they had Among Us, so we tried to play that, but not enough people joined, so we weren't able to get the game going. I then played song requests from YouTube while talking to chat about various things. I also played a few songs that I chose myself since chat kept requesting songs that I didn't personally like all that much. I also tried singing along to one of my favorite songs, "Green Fields of France" by Dropkick Murphys, which I had requested in Celes Aurion's stream earlier that day, but I had to leave her stream to start my own before we got to it in her queue. Towards the end of the song I actually got very emotional and started crying a little bit on stream, and I had to leave the room to go blow my nose. My viewers asked me if I was okay, and I explained to them that I was fine, the song just hit a very deep nerve for me and I couldn't hold back the tears, and one of them sent a text emote saying *hugs you through the screen*, which I felt was very touching. After that we played some music by Insane Clown Posse and the stream started to wind down as I ran out of things to talk about.
X (Twitter) Posting Notes:
Original Tweets/Posts: 2

Posted a tweet complaining about the tendency of corporate executives to layoff workers when their position is eliminated, when it's entirely possible to reassign workers to a new role without laying them off. This tweet got 15 views.

https://x.com/Maphesdus/status/2025232289668727041

Posted a second tweet focusing on my character's cybernetic eyes, which is a detail that isn't usually visible on IRIAM or Twitter due to image compression on both platforms. This tweet got 38 views.

https://x.com/Maphesdus/status/2025466270209224911

The first tweet was text only, and didn't have an image attached, while the second tweet did have an image. This tells me that people are more likely to view a tweet if it has an image.
X (Twitter) Interaction Notes:
Interactions (Likes, Replies, Retweets): ~68

For the interactions, I mostly entered into more art raffles. I've actually gotten a small amount of free art this way.
Reflection / Comments (Optional): I think I did a very good job of keeping dead air to a minimum during this stream (except for the last 10 minutes), and I kept engagement flowing along nicely. This is easier to do when there are more people active in chat. As for what I should focus on next, I think I still need to just focus on trying to maintain active dialogue with chat and keep things lively. I grew up in the 1990s and early 2000s, so the type of online interaction I'm most familiar with and most comfortable with is a forum style environment where everyone is communicating using text only, which makes it easier to have conversations where everyone is equal, and we can each reply at our own convenience, unbound by time constraints. I think part of the reason I've been struggling to get into streaming before now is that streaming kind of undoes both of those things. With streaming, all communication has to be done in real time. You can't come back and reply the next day and still expect to be part of the conversation. On top of that, the streamer is socially elevated above their chat, and even communicates using a different method (audio and video), which makes the streamer have to bear more of the burden of keeping the conversation flowing, whereas with forums, that burden can be more equally distributed among the person who initially posted a topic (often called the "original poster" or "OP" for short) and the people who reply to said topic. Because this is the format of online communication I grew up with and which I have been primarily using for the past 25+ years, I have never really developed the skills of monologuing when I don't have anyone else to talk to. But since it seems like the online world is moving more towards the streaming format of online communication, I guess I'll have to develop that skill if I want to keep up with the online world. I think being a streamer on IRIAM is definitely helping me to overcome that personal weakness of mine. I know a lot of streamers say they got into streaming because they couldn't stop talking, but for me it's the opposite. For me personally, being a streamer is like a training exercise that's helping me overcome my tendency to remain silent all the time. It hasn't been easy, but I think I'm definitely making improvements as I go along.
Which Viewer Support Practices did you a: I consciously focused on viewer interaction during my stream
Notes on Viewer Support Practices: I tried to focus on talking more to reduce dead air, and although I did successfully keep things pretty active for most of my stream, dead air did start to become a bit of a problem towards the end (like the last 10 minutes or so) as I ran out of things to talk about.
Current Viewer Support Focus Goal: Improve how I explain Fan Badges to new viewers

IRIAM Streaming Notes:
Stream Time: 1:35:38

This is the report for Saturday, February 21st. I thought I had submitted this report already last night, but it doesn't seem to have gone through, so I'm submitting it again. Anyway, on Saturday I made idle conversation with chat. I asked about their favorite video games and tried to find a video game we could all play together. A couple of people said they had Among Us, so we tried to play that, but not enough people joined, so we weren't able to get the game going. I then played song requests from YouTube while talking to chat about various things. I also played a few songs that I chose myself since chat kept requesting songs that I didn't personally like all that much. I also tried singing along to one of my favorite songs, "Green Fields of France" by Dropkick Murphys, which I had requested in Celes Aurion's stream earlier that day, but I had to leave her stream to start my own before we got to it in her queue. Towards the end of the song I actually got very emotional and started crying a little bit on stream, and I had to leave the room to go blow my nose. My viewers asked me if I was okay, and I explained to them that I was fine, the song just hit a very deep nerve for me and I couldn't hold back the tears, and one of them sent a text emote saying *hugs you through the screen*, which I felt was very touching. After that we played some music by Insane Clown Posse and the stream started to wind down as I ran out of things to talk about.
X (Twitter) Posting Notes:
Original Tweets/Posts: 2

Posted a tweet complaining about the tendency of corporate executives to layoff workers when their position is eliminated, when it's entirely possible to reassign workers to a new role without laying them off. This tweet got 15 views.

https://x.com/Maphesdus/status/2025232289668727041

Posted a second tweet focusing on my character's cybernetic eyes, which is a detail that isn't usually visible on IRIAM or Twitter due to image compression on both platforms. This tweet got 38 views.

https://x.com/Maphesdus/status/2025466270209224911

The first tweet was text only, and didn't have an image attached, while the second tweet did have an image. This tells me that people are more likely to view a tweet if it has an image.
X (Twitter) Interaction Notes:
Interactions (Likes, Replies, Retweets): ~68

For the interactions, I mostly entered into more art raffles. I've actually gotten a small amount of free art this way.
Reflection / Comments (Optional): I think I did a very good job of keeping dead air to a minimum during this stream (except for the last 10 minutes), and I kept engagement flowing along nicely. This is easier to do when there are more people active in chat. As for what I should focus on next, I think I still need to just focus on trying to maintain active dialogue with chat and keep things lively. I grew up in the 1990s and early 2000s, so the type of online interaction I'm most familiar with and most comfortable with is a forum style environment where everyone is communicating using text only, which makes it easier to have conversations where everyone is equal, and we can each reply at our own convenience, unbound by time constraints. I think part of the reason I've been struggling to get into streaming before now is that streaming kind of undoes both of those things. With streaming, all communication has to be done in real time. You can't come back and reply the next day and still expect to be part of the conversation. On top of that, the streamer is socially elevated above their chat, and even communicates using a different method (audio and video), which makes the streamer have to bear more of the burden of keeping the conversation flowing, whereas with forums, that burden can be more equally distributed among the person who initially posted a topic (often called the "original poster" or "OP" for short) and the people who reply to said topic. Because this is the format of online communication I grew up with and which I have been primarily using for the past 25+ years, I have never really developed the skills of monologuing when I don't have anyone else to talk to. But since it seems like the online world is moving more towards the streaming format of online communication, I guess I'll have to develop that skill if I want to keep up with the online world. I think being a streamer on IRIAM is definitely helping me to overcome that personal weakness of mine. I know a lot of streamers say they got into streaming because they couldn't stop talking, but for me it's the opposite. For me personally, being a streamer is like a training exercise that's helping me overcome my tendency to remain silent all the time. It hasn't been easy, but I think I'm definitely making improvements as I go along.
Which Viewer Support Practices did you a: I consciously focused on viewer interaction during my stream
Notes on Viewer Support Practices: I tried to focus on talking more to reduce dead air, and although I did successfully keep things pretty active for most of my stream, dead air did start to become a bit of a problem towards the end (like the last 10 minutes or so) as I ran out of things to talk about.
Current Viewer Support Focus Goal: Improve how I explain Fan Badges to new viewers
